title: Replace per-Session git watching with an app-global, repo-keyed GitMonitor (split signals + visible/busy gating)
context: Daily-drive lag traced to the git-watch layer. Log evidence over a 3-day window: ~12k git subprocess spawns, a 'Not a git repo' detection storm up to 96/min, dead-worktree refresh failures (170 [git] ERRORs against .claude/worktrees/* dirs deleted from disk, across OTHER projects), and 899 `gh pr view` calls (620 for `main` alone, 45 timing out on api.github.com). tian's own CPU stays flat at 0.1% â€” the lag is bursty subprocess-spawn contention on `.userInitiated`, not sustained compute, which is why it's intermittent (during builds / Claude file-writes / cwd churn). Root cause is architectural, not a bug: `SessionGitContext` is one-per-Session (Session.swift:201), so every watcher, PR cache (60s TTL), scheduler, and concurrency cap is per-session. The RefreshScheduler cap (debounce 250ms, maxConcurrent 2) is real but only gates the FSEvents react path AND only within one session â€” detection and `gh pr view` bypass it, and global concurrency is 2Ă—N sessions. Two sessions in one repo (the common sibling-worktree case) run duplicate watchers + duplicate caches + duplicate gh polling on the same paths. The trigger model compounds it: one recursive file-level FSEvents watcher over the whole working tree fires on every save/build-artifact, and each firing shells `git status` + branch + (Branch tab) `git log` + `gh pr view` â€” the noisiest signal driving the heaviest work, with network in the hot path. This does not reverse ADR 0004; it redesigns the git-context internals that survived the flatten.
decision: Move git watching out of Session into one app-global, repo-keyed `GitMonitor` (@MainActor @Observable, keyed by GitRepoID = git common-dir), and change the trigger model. Three coordinated changes: (A) OWNERSHIP â€” `GitMonitor` owns all watchers, the single shared `PRStatusCache`, one global `RefreshScheduler`, and global concurrency semaphores (a git-local lane + a separate gh-network lane). Sessions SUBSCRIBE by (repoID, worktreeRoot) with reference counting; a repo's watchers/caches are created on first subscriber and torn down (with tasks cancelled, disk-deletion GC'd) at refcount zero. `SessionGitContext` shrinks to a thin adapter: it keeps paneâ†’repo/worktreeRoot detection + subscription, and READS status from the monitor; the sidebar's `repoStatuses`/`statusByWorktreeRoot` read-shape is preserved so the view layer barely changes. (B) SPLIT THE SIGNAL by what actually changes each datum: a cheap always-on REFS watcher (`.git/HEAD` + `refs` + `packed-refs`; commonDir/refs + worktree gitDir for worktrees) drives branch name, branch-graph-dirty, and PR-cache eviction; the expensive whole-working-tree watcher drives ONLY the diff/dirty summary; PR state is driven by refs events (push/fetch) + a slow poll (TTL 60sâ†’300s) + on-gate-open, never by working-tree churn, with network backoff on failure. (C) GATE THE WORKING-TREE WATCHER â€” run it for a repo only while at least one subscribing session is VISIBLE (window on-screen per WindowVisibilityState + the active session in its workspace per `sessionIsVisible`) OR BUSY (its Claude pane state `resumesWork`, i.e. busy/active â€” so a background session actively writing files keeps its diff badge live). When the gate closes, stop the FSEvents stream entirely (not just skip the shell-out); on gate-open, do one eager catch-up refresh. The refs watcher and slow PR poll stay on regardless (cheap, keep branch/PR badges live for idle background sessions). Also: `detectRepo` gets a pathâ†’repoID? cache with a negative-result TTL and runs debounced through the global semaphore (kills the 'Not a git repo' storm), and `runGit` QoS drops `.userInitiated`â†’`.utility`. NOT doing libgit2/in-process git in this ADR â€” the spawn cost is secondary to duplication+scope; revisit only if A+B+C fall short.
consequences: Concurrency caps become real and global instead of 2Ă—N-sessions; duplicate watchers/caches/gh-polls for a shared repo collapse to one; dead worktrees GC automatically when their last subscriber drops (the 170-error leak disappears without a special-case). Working-tree FS churn stops driving branch/PR work, and the whole-tree watcher only runs for repos the user can see or that are actively working â€” bounding the expensive path to what matters. Trade-offs accepted: a session that is neither visible nor busy lets its diff/dirty badge go stale until it next becomes visible or busy (branch + PR stay live via the always-on cheap paths); PR state can lag up to ~5min for a change made outside a push/fetch. New global singleton lifecycle to get right (injection, teardown, refcount correctness under rapid session open/close and pane moves) â€” mitigated by keeping SessionGitContext's external read-shape stable so blast radius stays in the git layer. Enables later wins that were impossible per-session: a shared long-lived git process / fsmonitor, cross-session diff coalescing, and libgit2 as a drop-in behind GitMonitor. Ship order is layered: quick mitigations (QoS, PR TTL) first for immediate relief, then GitMonitor skeleton + global concurrency, split watchers, gating, and finally SessionGitContext-as-adapter + detection cache.
staysTrue: ADR 0004's Workspaceâ†’Session model is unchanged; the sidebar still shows per-session branch + diff + PR badges with the same semantics. Ghostty still owns the terminal core; git status still comes from the git CLI (this ADR does not adopt libgit2). ADR 0002 holds â€” this is app internals, not new `tian` binary subcommands or IPC surface. Remote repos keep their PollingRefresher path (FSEvents can't cross hosts); GitMonitor subsumes it as the per-repo poll rather than replacing the mechanism. The existing occlusion gating for decorative animations (WindowVisibilityState) is reused, not duplicated.
